examples.da: Der er helt stille i huset.
examples.en: It is completely quiet in the house.
Be quiet, the child is sleeping.

stille.gotcha: Also a verb meaning 'to place/put' (stille et spørgsmål = ask a question).

examples.da: Der er kun én ting, jeg mangler.
examples.en: There is only one thing I am missing.

examples.da: Én af os må blive hjemme.
examples.en: One of us has to stay home.

én.gotcha: The accent stresses the number 'one', distinguishing it from the article 'en' (a/an).

examples.en: We meet at five o'clock.
She has five siblings.
When does the train arrive?
When is your birthday?

hvornår.gotcha: Only asks about time. In statements use 'når' or 'da' for 'when', never hvornår.

examples.en: All plants die without water.

examples.da: Han døde for ti år siden.
examples.en: He died ten years ago.

dø.gotcha: Past tense is 'døde', past participle 'død'. Present 'dør' looks like the noun 'dør' (door).

examples.en: That is the correct answer.

examples.da: Hun er en rigtig god ven.
examples.en: She is a really good friend.

rigtig.gotcha: As adverb 'rigtig(t)' often just means 'really/very', not 'correctly'.

examples.da: Jeg er så glad i dag.
examples.en: I am so happy today.
She will be happy about the gift.

glad.gotcha: 'Glad for' means happy about; 'glad i' something can mean fond of it. The d is soft.

examples.en: Everything is fine.

examples.da: Hun holder orden på sit værelse.
examples.en: She keeps her room tidy.

orden.gotcha: 'I orden' is a very common fixed phrase meaning 'okay/all right'.

examples.en: Remember to buy milk.

examples.da: Jeg kan ikke huske hans navn.
examples.en: I can't remember his name.

huske.gotcha: Usually with 'kan': 'jeg kan ikke huske' = I don't remember.

examples.en: I don't live here anymore.

examples.da: Vejen er længere, end jeg troede.
examples.en: The road is longer than I thought.

længere.gotcha: With 'ikke' it means 'anymore'; otherwise it is the comparative 'longer/farther'.

examples.en: These books are mine.
Have you read these articles?

disse.gotcha: Most often the plural demonstrative 'these' (of denne/dette), not the slang verb.

examples.en: Goodbye, see you tomorrow!

examples.da: Hun sagde farvel til sine venner.
examples.en: She said goodbye to her friends.
He hit the ball hard.
We beat them at football.

slå.gotcha: Irregular: slår, slog, slået. Many phrasal uses: slå op (look up), slå til (accept).

examples.en: Both my parents work.

examples.da: Vi kan ikke vælge begge dele.
examples.en: We can't choose both.

begge.gotcha: For 'both things' Danish says 'begge dele', not just 'begge'.

examples.da: Vi kører i bil til arbejde.
examples.en: We drive to work by car.

examples.da: Han har købt en ny bil.
examples.en: He has bought a new car.
The cat killed a mouse.
No one was killed in the accident.

dræbe.gotcha: Past tense is 'dræbte'. Not the same as 'slå ihjel', though both mean to kill.

examples.en: They love each other.
We help each other with the homework.

examples.da: Hørt! Det er jeg helt enig i.
examples.en: Hear, hear! I completely agree.
Have you heard the news?

hørt.gotcha: Also simply the past participle of 'høre' (to hear).

examples.da: Jeg har læst en del af bogen.
examples.en: I have read part of the book.

examples.da: En stor del af eleverne var syge.
examples.en: A large part of the students were sick.

del.gotcha: 'En del' alone often means 'quite a lot', not just 'a part'.

examples.da: De har et barn på tre år.
examples.en: They have a three-year-old child.
The children are playing in the garden.

barn.gotcha: Irregular plural with vowel change: barn → børn.

examples.da: Jeg lavede en fejl i opgaven.
examples.en: I made a mistake in the assignment.
It was my fault.

fejl.gotcha: Same form in singular and plural: en fejl, flere fejl.

examples.en: The knight takes the pawn on e5.

S.gotcha: In chess notation S stands for 'springer' (knight), not a word starting with S.

examples.en: See you later today.

examples.da: Kan vi tale om det senere?
examples.en: Can we talk about it later?

examples.da: Der står en kvinde ved døren.
examples.en: There is a woman standing at the door.
She is a strong woman.

kvinde.gotcha: The v is pronounced; roughly 'KVEN-eh'. Plural is 'kvinder'.

examples.en: The station is five minutes from here.
We can see the sea from here.

examples.da: Vi er fire personer til middag.
examples.en: We are four people for dinner.
The train leaves in four minutes.

fire.gotcha: False friend: looks like English 'fire' but means 'four'. Pronounced roughly 'FEE-ah'.

examples.en: The bishop moves to c4.

L.gotcha: In chess notation L stands for 'løber' (bishop), literally 'runner'.

examples.en: Close the door, please.
Someone is knocking on the door.

dør.gotcha: Identical to 'dør' (dies), present tense of 'dø'. Context decides the meaning.

examples.en: What the hell are you doing?
The meeting was pure hell.

helvede.gotcha: Common swear word; 'for helvede' is quite strong in Danish.

examples.da: De bor i et gult hus.
examples.en: They live in a yellow house.
The house has a big garden.

hus.gotcha: Et-word: et hus, huset. Plural is 'huse'.

examples.da: Tag det roligt, vi har god tid.
examples.en: Take it easy, we have plenty of time.
It is a quiet street.

rolig.gotcha: The g is silent; sounds like 'ROH-li'. 'Bare rolig' means 'don't worry'.

examples.en: She runs very fast.
Come quickly, it's urgent!

hurtigt.gotcha: The g is silent; pronounced roughly 'HOR-tit'.

examples.da: Gå lige frem og drej til højre.
examples.en: Go straight ahead and turn right.
She took out her phone.

frem.gotcha: 'Frem' is movement forward; 'fremme' is being at the front/arrived. Danish distinguishes them.

examples.da: Jeg kan godt klare det selv.
examples.en: I can manage it myself.
She passed the exam without problems.

klare.gotcha: 'Klare sig' means to cope/do well: 'han klarer sig godt' = he is doing well.

examples.en: The bus runs every ten minutes.

examples.da: Min far kører mig i skole.
examples.en: My dad drives me to school.

kører.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'køre' (drives/runs); the noun 'driver' is rarer.

examples.da: Jeg har fået et nyt job.
examples.en: I have got a new job.
Are you new here?

ny.gotcha: Inflects: ny, nyt, nye. 'På ny' means 'anew/again'.

examples.da: Jeg kan heller ikke lide fisk.
examples.en: I don't like fish either.

examples.da: Hun kommer heller ikke i morgen.
examples.en: She isn't coming tomorrow either.

heller.gotcha: Almost always in 'heller ikke' (not either). Don't confuse with 'hellere' (rather).

examples.en: May I ask a question?
That is a good question.

spørgsmål.gotcha: You 'stille' a question in Danish, not 'spørge' it. Same form in plural.

examples.en: It is far to the beach.

examples.da: Vi er nået langt i dag.
examples.en: We have come far today.

langt.gotcha: 'Langt' is distance (far); 'længe' is duration (a long time). Learners often mix them.

examples.da: Vi gik en tur rundt om søen.
examples.en: We took a walk around the lake.
The children are running around in the garden.

examples.da: Min bror er to år ældre end mig.
examples.en: My brother is two years older than me.
Do you have brothers or sisters?

bror.gotcha: Irregular plural with vowel change: bror → brødre.

examples.en: I am almost done.
There were almost no people.

examples.da: Hvilken farve kan du bedst lide?
examples.en: Which colour do you like best?
Which day suits you?

hvilken.gotcha: Agrees with gender/number: hvilken (en), hvilket (et), hvilke (plural).

examples.da: Husk dit pas, når du rejser.
examples.en: Remember your passport when you travel.
My passport expires next year.

pas.gotcha: Also the imperative of 'passe': 'pas på!' means 'watch out!'.

examples.en: She is the head of the department.

examples.da: Vi har fået en ny leder.
examples.en: We have got a new manager.

leder.gotcha: Also the present tense of 'lede': 'jeg leder efter...' = I am looking for...

examples.da: Du bør tale med en læge.
examples.en: You should talk to a doctor.
One should not smoke indoors.

bør.gotcha: In practice almost always the modal verb 'should' (from 'burde'), not the rare nouns.

examples.da: Butikken har åbent indtil klokken otte.
examples.en: The shop is open until eight o'clock.

examples.da: Vent her, indtil jeg kommer tilbage.
examples.en: Wait here until I come back.
Open the book to page ten.

examples.da: Han satte sig ved min side.
examples.en: He sat down by my side.

side.gotcha: Also means 'page'. Pronounced with soft d, unlike English 'side'.

examples.da: Ring til mig inden klokken fem.
examples.en: Call me before five o'clock.
Wash your hands before you eat.

inden.gotcha: Means 'before (a deadline)'; 'før' is more general. Don't confuse with 'inde' (inside).

examples.en: I would rather stay home.

examples.da: Vil du hellere have te end kaffe?
examples.en: Would you rather have tea than coffee?

hellere.gotcha: Don't confuse with 'heller' in 'heller ikke' (not either).

examples.en: The phone is ringing, please answer it.

examples.da: Jeg ringer til dig i aften.
examples.en: I will call you tonight.

ringer.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'ringe' (calls/rings); the noun 'bell ringer' is rare.

examples.da: Jeg tager et bad hver morgen.
examples.en: I take a shower every morning.
The apartment has a small bathroom.

bad.gotcha: False friend: not English 'bad'. Also the past tense of 'bede' (asked).

examples.en: I am against the proposal.

examples.da: Har du noget imod, at jeg åbner vinduet?
examples.en: Do you mind if I open the window?

imod.gotcha: 'Have noget imod' means 'to mind', a very common fixed phrase.

examples.da: Lad os gå videre til næste punkt.
examples.en: Let's move on to the next item.

examples.da: Hun sendte beskeden videre til chefen.
examples.en: She forwarded the message to the boss.

videre.gotcha: 'Ikke videre' means 'not particularly': 'ikke videre godt' = not very good.

examples.da: Jeg tænker på dig hver dag.
examples.en: I think about you every day.
What are you thinking about?

tænker.gotcha: Usually present tense of 'tænke' (thinks); the noun 'thinker' is rarer.

examples.en: That is such crap!
The movie was absolute rubbish.

lort.gotcha: Vulgar but very common in speech; milder in Danish than 'shit' feels in English.

examples.da: Jeg har ingenting at lave i dag.
examples.en: I have nothing to do today.
Nothing happened.

examples.da: Hun lavede mad, mens jeg dækkede bord.
examples.en: She cooked while I set the table.

examples.da: Vent her, mens jeg parkerer bilen.
examples.en: Wait here while I park the car.
Sorry, we are closed.
I apologize for the delay.

beklager.gotcha: More formal apology; for bumping into someone Danes say 'undskyld'.

examples.en: Damn, I forgot my keys!
That is nonsense.

pis.gotcha: Vulgar but everyday; 'tage pis på nogen' means to pull someone's leg.

examples.en: The child lost its toy.
He sold his old house.

sit.gotcha: Reflexive: refers back to the subject. Use hans/hendes when the owner is someone else.

examples.en: That is not a problem.

examples.da: Vi har et lille problem med bilen.
examples.en: We have a small problem with the car.

problem.gotcha: Et-word despite ending in a 'foreign' way: et problem, problemet.

examples.en: What does that word mean?
He kept his word.

ord.gotcha: The d is silent; sounds like 'oar'. Same form in plural: et ord, mange ord.

examples.en: The cake is very sweet.
Your sister is really nice.

sød.gotcha: About people it means 'nice/kind/cute', not 'sweet-tasting'. Soft d at the end.

examples.da: De har fået en lille dreng.
examples.en: They have had a baby boy.
The boys are playing football in the park.

examples.da: Vi kom sent hjem i går.
examples.en: We got home late yesterday.

examples.da: Det er for sent at ringe nu.
examples.en: It is too late to call now.

sent.gotcha: False friend: not English 'sent'. 'For sent' means 'too late'.

examples.da: Det var rigtig sjovt i går.
examples.en: It was really fun yesterday.

examples.da: Sjovt nok mødte jeg ham igen.
examples.en: Funnily enough, I met him again.

sjovt.gotcha: 'Sjov' means fun/amusing, not strange; 'sjovt nok' = funnily enough.

examples.en: I bought a set of towels.

examples.da: Hun vandt første sæt i tennis.
examples.en: She won the first set in tennis.

sæt.gotcha: Also the imperative of 'sætte': 'sæt dig ned' = sit down.

examples.da: Hr. Lang bor på anden sal.
examples.en: Mr. Lang lives on the second floor.
It was a long day.

examples.da: Vejen til byen er meget lang.
examples.en: The road to town is very long.

lang.gotcha: For time use 'længe' (adverb): 'hvor længe?' = how long (time)?

examples.da: Hun har fået et nyt job.
examples.en: She has got a new job.
I am looking for a job in Copenhagen.

job.gotcha: Et-word: et job, jobbet. Plural is also 'job' or 'jobs'.

examples.en: The children are inside the house.

examples.da: Det er koldt, lad os blive inde.
examples.en: It is cold, let's stay inside.

inde.gotcha: 'Inde' is static location; 'ind' is movement into. Danish strictly distinguishes them.

examples.da: Deres datter går i skole nu.
examples.en: Their daughter goes to school now.
She is my oldest daughter.

examples.da: Sæt en timer på ti minutter.
examples.en: Set a timer for ten minutes.

examples.da: Ovnens timer bipper, når maden er klar.
examples.en: The oven's timer beeps when the food is ready.

timer.gotcha: Also the plural of 'time' (hour): 'to timer' = two hours. Context decides.

examples.da: Vi har prøve i matematik i morgen.
examples.en: We have a maths test tomorrow.

examples.da: Vil du prøve mine nye ski?
examples.en: Do you want to try my new skis?

prøve.gotcha: As verb it means 'to try': 'prøv at smage' = try tasting it.

examples.da: Det er svært at lære dansk udtale.
examples.en: It is hard to learn Danish pronunciation.

examples.da: Hun havde svært ved at sove.
examples.en: She had trouble sleeping.

svært.gotcha: 'Have svært ved' = to have difficulty with; a very common fixed phrase.

examples.da: Kan du køre mig til stationen?
examples.en: Can you drive me to the station?

examples.da: Vi kører til Aarhus i weekenden.
examples.en: We are driving to Aarhus this weekend.

køre.gotcha: Used for any wheeled travel, even as passenger; irregular past 'kørte'.

examples.da: Har du nogensinde været i Danmark?
examples.en: Have you ever been to Denmark?

examples.da: Det er den bedste kage, jeg nogensinde har smagt.
examples.en: It is the best cake I have ever tasted.
The cat is hiding behind the sofa.
Who is behind the plan?

bag.gotcha: False friend: not English 'bag' (that is 'taske'). Also a noun meaning 'behind/bottom'.

examples.da: Vi snakkede i telefon i en time.
examples.en: We talked on the phone for an hour.

examples.da: Kan vi snakke om det senere?
examples.en: Can we talk about it later?

snakke.gotcha: Informal everyday word for 'tale'; completely neutral in Danish, not negative.

examples.en: Are you done with your homework?

examples.da: Maden er færdig om fem minutter.
examples.en: The food is ready in five minutes.

færdig.gotcha: 'Færdig med' = finished with. The g is silent: sounds like 'FAIR-di'.

examples.da: Kan du vise mig vejen til stationen?
examples.en: Can you show me the way to the station?
She showed us her holiday photos.

vise.gotcha: Also a noun meaning 'ballad/song' (en vise). Past tense is 'viste'.

examples.en: The movie starts at ten o'clock.

examples.da: Der er ti æbler i posen.
examples.en: There are ten apples in the bag.

ti.gotcha: Also the imperative of 'tie': 'ti stille!' = be quiet!

examples.en: Mr. Jensen is waiting in the meeting room.

examples.da: Kære hr. Hansen, tak for Deres brev.
examples.en: Dear Mr. Hansen, thank you for your letter.

hr..gotcha: Mostly formal or written; Danes normally use first names, even with strangers.

examples.en: She has her own apartment.
He started his own company.

egen.gotcha: Inflects: egen (en), eget (et), egne (plural). Usually after min/din/sin.

examples.en: How old are you?

examples.da: De bor i et gammelt hus.
examples.en: They live in an old house.

gammel.gotcha: Drops an m when inflected: gammel → gamle. Comparative is irregular: ældre, ældst.

examples.en: I am staying home tonight.
Are your parents home?

hjemme.gotcha: 'Hjemme' is being at home; 'hjem' is going home. The h is silent.

examples.da: Vi kørte forbi din skole i dag.
examples.en: We drove past your school today.
The summer is almost over.

forbi.gotcha: 'Komme forbi' means to drop by/visit, not to pass by.

examples.en: The weather was fantastic all week.

examples.da: Hvor er det fantastisk at se dig!
examples.en: How wonderful to see you!

examples.da: Det er dyrt, men dog en god investering.
examples.en: It is expensive, but still a good investment.
How beautiful it is here!

dog.gotcha: False friend: not English 'dog' (that is 'hund'). Often just adds emphasis in exclamations.

examples.da: Jeg skal bruge et bor til væggen.
examples.en: I need a drill bit for the wall.
The drill bit broke in the middle of the work.

bor.gotcha: Identical to 'bor' (lives), present tense of 'bo': 'jeg bor i København'.

examples.en: The food tastes really good.

examples.da: Skal vi lave mad sammen i aften?
examples.en: Shall we cook together tonight?

mad.gotcha: False friend: not English 'mad'. Soft d: sounds roughly like 'madh'.

examples.en: I have the day off on Friday.
Is this seat free?

fri.gotcha: 'Have fri' means having time off work. As a verb it means to propose marriage.

examples.en: Let me think about it.

examples.da: Jeg tænkte på dig i går.
examples.en: I thought about you yesterday.

tænke.gotcha: 'Tænke på' = think about; 'tænke over' = consider. 'Synes' is used for opinions.

examples.en: The bus stops right by the school.

examples.da: Du skal stoppe med at ryge.
examples.en: You have to stop smoking.

stoppe.gotcha: 'Stoppe med at' + infinitive = stop doing something. Also means to stuff/darn.

examples.da: John kommer til festen på lørdag.
examples.en: John is coming to the party on Saturday.
We walked through the park.

examples.da: Jeg fik jobbet gennem en ven.
examples.en: I got the job through a friend.

gennem.gotcha: The g in the middle is soft; sounds roughly like 'GEN-em'.

examples.da: Jeg føler mig træt i dag.
examples.en: I feel tired today.
She feels at home here.

føler.gotcha: Usually present tense of 'føle'; needs reflexive 'sig': 'føle sig' = to feel.

examples.da: Der bor omkring tusind mennesker i byen.
examples.en: About a thousand people live in the town.

examples.da: Vi sad omkring bordet og snakkede.
examples.en: We sat around the table talking.
We went in through the back door.
She laughed at his joke.

ad.gotcha: Easily confused with 'af'; both often sound alike. 'Le ad' = laugh at.

examples.da: Hvad kommer der til at ske nu?
examples.en: What is going to happen now?
The accident happened last night.

ske.gotcha: Also a noun meaning 'spoon' (en ske). Past tense is 'skete'.

examples.en: The train goes through the tunnel.

examples.da: Vi kom igennem en hård tid sammen.
examples.en: We got through a hard time together.
The house is located between two lakes.

examples.da: Det er en hemmelighed mellem os.
examples.en: It is a secret between us.
Welcome to Copenhagen!

examples.da: Du er altid velkommen hos os.
examples.en: You are always welcome at our place.

velkommen.gotcha: 'You're welcome' after thanks is 'selv tak' or 'det var så lidt', never 'velkommen'.

examples.da: Hold kæft, hvor var det sjovt!
examples.en: Wow, that was so funny!
The dog opened its big jaws.

kæft.gotcha: 'Hold kæft' means 'shut up' but also expresses amazement; rude about a person's mouth.

examples.da: Vi måtte vente i to timer.
examples.en: We had to wait for two hours.
Wipe your feet on the mat.

måtte.gotcha: Past tense of 'må', covering both 'had to' and 'was allowed to'. Context decides.

examples.en: I will call you later.

examples.da: Kan du ringe efter en taxa?
examples.en: Can you call for a taxi?

ringe.gotcha: As adjective it means 'poor/inferior': 'en ringe kvalitet' = poor quality.

examples.da: Jeg hader at stå tidligt op.
examples.en: I hate getting up early.
She hates cobwebs.

hader.gotcha: Usually present tense of 'hade' (hates); the noun 'a hater' is informal slang.

examples.da: Jack er lige flyttet til Danmark.
examples.en: Jack has just moved to Denmark.

examples.da: Hun vandt løbet i sidste weekend.
examples.en: She won the race last weekend.
He ran all the way home.

løb.gotcha: Also the past tense of 'løbe' (ran). 'I løbet af' = during/in the course of.

examples.da: Der står en mand ved døren.
examples.en: There is a man standing at the door.
What does the letter say?

stå.gotcha: Irregular: står, stod, stået. 'Der står...' is how Danish says a text 'says' something.

examples.en: We eat dinner at six o'clock.

examples.da: Har du lyst til at spise med?
examples.en: Would you like to join us for dinner?
Wait a moment, I am coming now.
We are expecting guests tonight.

vente.gotcha: Means both 'wait' and 'expect': 'vente barn' = to be expecting a baby.

examples.en: Give him another chance.

examples.da: Der er en god chance for sol i morgen.
examples.en: There is a good chance of sun tomorrow.

chance.gotcha: Pronounced the French way, roughly 'SJANG-se', not like English 'chance'.

examples.en: I am not tired at all.

examples.da: Det var slet ikke så svært.
examples.en: It was not that hard at all.

slet.gotcha: In modern Danish almost only in 'slet ikke' (not at all).

examples.da: Slap af, det skal nok gå.
examples.en: Relax, it will be fine.
The elastic has become completely loose.

slap.gotcha: 'Slap af!' (relax!) is very common. Also the past tense of 'slippe' (let go).

examples.da: Politiet fandt et våben i bilen.
examples.en: The police found a weapon in the car.

examples.da: Det er forbudt at bære våben.
examples.en: It is illegal to carry weapons.

våben.gotcha: Same form in singular and plural: et våben, flere våben.

examples.da: Bogen handler om en ung pige.
examples.en: The book is about a young girl.

examples.da: Det handler om at være ærlig.
examples.en: It is about being honest.

handler.gotcha: Most often the verb 'handle om' (be about) or 'handle' (shop/act), not the noun 'trader'.

examples.da: Han gav hende en ring med en diamant.
examples.en: He gave her a ring with a diamond.

examples.da: Børnene stod i en ring om læreren.
examples.en: The children stood in a circle around the teacher.

ring.gotcha: Also the imperative of 'ringe': 'ring til mig!' = call me!

examples.da: Det passer ikke, hvad han siger.
examples.en: What he says is not true.
The dress fits you perfectly.

passer.gotcha: Usually the verb 'passe' (fit, suit, be true, look after); the compass tool is rare.

examples.en: Which language do you speak best?

examples.da: Han kom for sent, hvilket irriterede alle.
examples.en: He was late, which annoyed everyone.

hvilket.gotcha: Used with et-words, and to refer back to a whole clause ('which').

examples.en: The story's narrator is a child.
She always tells good stories.

fortæller.gotcha: Also present tense of 'fortælle' (tells). Context decides noun or verb.

examples.da: Hvem har vundet flest kampe og scoret mest?
examples.en: Who has won the most matches and scored the most?

examples.da: Jeg kan bedst lide sommeren, mest på grund af lyset.
examples.en: I like summer best, mostly because of the light.

mest.gotcha: 'Mest' is for quantity/degree; 'flest' is for countable things (most people = flest mennesker).

examples.da: Hun brugte al sin tid på projektet.
examples.en: She spent all her time on the project.
All the food was eaten.
We live close to the station.

examples.da: Der var tæt trafik hele morgenen.
examples.en: There was heavy traffic all morning.

tæt.gotcha: 'Tæt på' = close to. Also means dense or watertight depending on context.
